title: Southern chess pie
subtitle: An American custard pie thickened with cornmeal and a splash of vinegar
excerpt: Chess pie is a southern American custard pie with a filling of butter, sugar, eggs, a small amount of cornmeal, cream, and a splash of white wine vinegar. It is intensely sweet, very rich, and sets to a firm, sliceable custard with a slightly caramelised top.
sourceNotes: Chess pie is documented in Southern American household cookbooks from the nineteenth century, including public-domain publications from the Reconstruction era. The inclusion of cornmeal and vinegar in a sweet custard pie is distinctive to Southern American baking. The form here follows the standard home-baking tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pie must be completely cool before slicing, at least 3 hours. It keeps at room temperature for 2 days, or in the fridge for 4 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 155 degrees C; the top will crack slightly and turn golden brown

glossaryTerms.definition: A small amount of white wine vinegar is added to chess pie filling not for flavour but for chemistry: the acidity helps the eggs set more firmly and evenly, reducing the risk of the custard weeping. The amount is small enough that the vinegar itself is imperceptible in the finished pie. Chess pie is intensely sweet; without the slight acidity from the vinegar, the filling can feel one-dimensional.

content.text: Chess pie is an exercise in excess: the filling is almost entirely butter, sugar, and eggs, thickened slightly with a little cornmeal and cream. The cornmeal gives the set custard a faint graininess and prevents it from being too smooth. The

sounds strange but is essential: a tablespoon of white wine vinegar helps the custard set cleanly and balances the extreme sweetness. The pie must cool completely before slicing; cutting it warm produces a liquid filling.

items.prepNote: 2 to 3 tbsp cold, for the pastry

content.text: Combine the flour, icing sugar, and salt. Rub in the cold butter to a coarse breadcrumb texture. Add cold water a tablespoon at a time, mixing until the dough just comes together. Wrap and chill for 30 minutes. Preheat the oven to 200 degrees C (conventional). Roll the pastry to line a 23 cm pie dish, pressing it into the sides and leaving an overhang. Line with baking paper, fill with baking beans, and blind-bake for 15 minutes. Remove the beans and paper and bake for a further 5 minutes until the base is pale golden. Reduce the oven to 175 degrees C (conventional).

Whisk together the caster sugar and melted butter. Add the eggs one at a time, whisking well. Add the double cream, cornmeal, vanilla, and vinegar and whisk until smooth. Pour into the warm pastry case. Bake at 175 degrees C for 40 to 45 minutes, until the top is golden brown and the filling has a very slight wobble in the centre but is set around the edges. Cover the pastry rim loosely with strips of foil if it is darkening too fast. Cool in the dish on a rack for at least 3 hours before slicing.
